content.full_text:
RAW MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
September 6,
1995
Kathryn A. Marshall
Assistant City Clerk-Treasurer
City of Whittier
13230 Penn Street
Whittier,
CA 90602—1772
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Assistance
Our File No.
1—95—263
Dear Ms. Marshall:
You have requested confirmation of telephone assistance
provided to you concerning the conflict of interest disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
!
Since you are asking a
general question,
we consider your letter to be a reguest for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c)./
You asked if the Whittier Redevelopment Agency
(“Agency”) is
required to adopt a conflict of interest code.
Your letter states
that the Agency is a separate legal body from the City of
Whittier; the boundaries of the Agency are within the boundaries
of the City of Whittier; the City Council serves as the body that
sets policy for the Agency; and there are usually one or two
consultants under contract directly to the Agency that do not work
for the City.
During our telephone conversation on August 28,
1995,
you also advised that the Agency was created by an ordinance
adopted by the City Council, but the Agency has its own budget and
rules of procedures.
Additionally,
you stated that the City’s
conflict of interest code includes “consultants” in the list of
designated positions.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
&q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an -opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800. SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Ms. Marshall
Our File No.
1—95—263
Page Two
The Commission has consistently advised that a redevelopment
agency which is comprised solely of the city’s councilmembers is
not required to develop a separate conflict of interest code.
(Valdez Advice Letter,
No.
1-89-601 and Briggs Advice Letter,
No.
A—87-289, copies enclosed) Qwever, we have also advised
that any other officials or employees who have decisioninaking
V
authority for the Agency should be included in a conflict of
interest code.
V
As the code reviewing body for conflict of interest codes
within the City’s jurisdiction, the City Council has the
discretion to include the Agency’s designated employees and
disclosure categories within the City’s conflictof interest code
rather than require promulgation of a separate conflict of
interest code for the Agency.
(Section 82011, Valdez, supra.)
Therefore, you may amend the City’s conflict of interest code to
include Agency consultants in the list of designated positions.
Alternatively, if you determine that the Agency is part of
the City’s governmental structure
since the City Council initially
adopted the ordinance to create the Agency, it is not necessary
for the City’s code to be amended since the consultant designation
is currently included.
The authority to require disclosure of
economic interests from consultants under contract directly to the
Agency already exists in the City’s conflict of interest code.
If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
by:• Dixie Howard
V Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question: Is the Whittier Redevelopment Agency required to adopt a conflict of interest code?
sections.conclusion: The Commission has consistently advised that a redevelopment agency which is comprised solely of the city's councilmembers is not required to develop a separate conflict of interest code. However, any other officials or employees who have decision-making authority for the Agency should be included in a conflict of interest code. The City Council may amend the City's conflict of interest code to include Agency consultants rather than require a separate code, or if the Agency is part of the City's governmental structure, the existing consultant designation in the City's code is sufficient.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a redevelopment agency that is a separate legal entity but governed by the city council and has consultants under contract must adopt its own conflict of interest code under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Whittier Redevelopment Agency is not required to adopt a separate conflict of interest code since it is comprised of city councilmembers. The City can either amend its existing code to explicitly include Agency consultants or rely on the existing consultant designation already in the City's code.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter responding to a telephone assistance request. The document contains clear question and conclusion sections. Minor note: document has small typo 'reguest' for 'request' in original text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is the Whittier Redevelopment Agency required to adopt a conflict of interest code?

CONCLUSION: The Commission has consistently advised that a redevelopment agency which is comprised solely of the city's councilmembers is not required to develop a separate conflict of interest code. However, any other officials or employees who have decision-making authority for the Agency should be included in a conflict of interest code. The City Council may amend the City's conflict of interest code to include Agency consultants rather than require a separate code, or if the Agency is part of the City's governmental structure, the existing consultant designation in the City's code is sufficient.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms that the Whittier Redevelopment Agency, composed of city councilmembers, does not need a separate conflict of interest code but Agency consultants should be covered under the City's existing or amended code.
